narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated an 83.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile includes being a full-time female student from a middle-income family, with a White racial background, an undergraduate GPA of 3.20, an LSAT score of 35, and notably low rankings in your school's grade deciles—placing you in the 2nd decile for both Year 1 and Year 3 performance, compared to typical students who average around the 5th or 6th decile. These details stood out when the model assessed your likelihood of bar success. The strongest influence on this prediction came from your Year 3 grade decile, which significantly pushed the model toward expecting a failure. Right behind that was your Year 1 grade decile, also nudging the prediction in the same unfavorable direction. Your LSAT score followed closely as the next key factor, contributing further to the high failure risk. Other elements like your family income bracket, undergraduate GPA, racial background, gender, and full-time status played supporting roles, all tilting the scales toward failure rather than success—none strongly argued for passing in your case. In comparison, typical admitted students have higher grade deciles, LSAT scores around 37, and GPAs just above 3.2, which help them clear this hurdle. While your undergraduate GPA was very close to average and your demographics align with most students, your lower early and later law school rankings and slightly below-average LSAT were the standout concerns. To improve your chances next time, focus on boosting your academic performance in school to climb those decile rankings through better study habits or tutoring, as that's the top factor here. Retaking the LSAT to aim above 37 could make a big difference too, since it's a close third in influence. With targeted prep for the bar itself mirroring these improvements, you could shift future predictions in your favor and strengthen your application. Keep pushing—you're close on some metrics, and these changes are realistic steps forward.
